title: Systems, methods and devices for communication
abstract: Devices, systems, and methods for training both non-humans and humans to communicate more effectively than previously possible. In one aspect a system for communicating with an animal comprises a plurality of tiles operably connected to one another, each tile comprising one or more buttons, an infrared transmitter and receiver assembly, a microprocessor, a speaker controlled by the microprocessor, and an audio amplifier connected to the speaker, where the infrared transmitter and receiver assembly transfers information to and from at least one other of the plurality of tiles, and where when one of the one or more buttons are pushed by the animal, the speaker emits a sound that can be heard through the speaker by the animal.
claims: Claims CLAIMS What is claimed is: 1. A system for communicating with an animal, the system comprising: a plurality of tiles operably connected to one another, each tile comprising: one or more buttons; an infrared transmitter and receiver assembly; a microprocessor; a speaker controlled by the microprocessor; and an audio amplifier operably connected to the speaker; where the infrared transmitter and receiver assembly transfers information to and from at least one other of the plurality of tiles; and where when one of the one or more buttons are pushed by the animal, the speaker emits a sound that can be heard through the speaker by the animal. 2. The system of claim 1 , further comprising a network connection device connected to the plurality of tiles and a computing device, where the network connection device connects the computing device to an internet or cloud and where the computing device receives or sends data via the internet or cloud to or from at least one of the plurality of tiles. 3. The system of claim 1, where each of the buttons comprise: a microswitch actuated by a press of the button; a force sensor; and a light emitter; where the microswitch provides feedback to the animal indicating it has been pressed. 4. The system of claim 3, where the force sensor is a resistive force sensor. 5. The system of claim 3, where the light emitters are RGB LEDâs. 6. The system of claim 1, where the information transferred includes at least one of location data, credentials, data generated by the button and status of components. 62 7. A layout detection system, comprising: a plurality of hextiles, each of the plurality of hextiles comprising: an infrared transmitter that transmits a unique code; an infrared receiver; and a radio transmitter; and a receiver that determines the layout of tiles based on the unique codes; and where each radio transmitter transmits data to at least one component of its respective hextile based upon the layout 8. The layout detection system of claim 7, where the radio transmitter is a near field computing transmitter. 9. The layout detection system of claim 7, where the radio transmitter is a Bluetooth Low Energy transmitter. 10. The layout detection system of claim 7, where the radio transmitter is a WiFi transmitter. 11. The layout detection system of claim 7, where the radio transmitter is a cellular network. 12. The layout detection system of claim 7, where the component of the hextile is a button. 13. The layout detection system of claim 7, where the component of the hextile is a receiver mounted on the hextile. 14. The layout detection system of claim 7, where the infrared transmitter and infrared receiver identify one or more animals in proximity to the system. 63 15. An expandable tile system for communicating with animals, the system comprising: a plurality of hextiles operably connected to each other, each of the plurality of hextiles comprising: an infrared transmitter; an infrared receiver; a radio transmitter; and a speaker; and where each radio transmitter transmits data to at least one component of its respective hextile; and where the plurality of tiles comprise moveable button assemblies that allow buttons to move in or out from a center. 16. The tile system of claim 15, where the plurality of hextiles is operably connected to one another with magnets. 17. The tile system of claim 15, where the moveable button assemblies are controlled by motors. 18. The tile system of claim 15, where the moveable button assemblies are controlled by an adjustment device and one or more mechanical linkages. 19. The tile system of claim 18, where the adjustment device causes the button assemblies to move a substantially similar distance from the center of the tile system. 20. The tile system of claim 15, where the adjustment device has a sizing knob with a plurality of click in pace positions. 64
